## Context

First iteration of the freshly-extracted `Cech-Cohomology` subproject. Goal: prove the
protected `AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (`lem:cech_computes_cohomology`)
and its cone, zero sorry / zero axioms. At start: 3 inline sorries (`CechNerve`,
`CechAcyclic.affine`, `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`), all blocked on absent-from-Mathlib
infrastructure; inherited strategy routed the comparison theorem through TWO spectral
sequences for `Scheme.Modules`. Frontier was empty ("Ready to prove: none").

Ran a 4-way parallel read-only validation pass before committing any prover budget:
strategy-critic, strategy-auditor (read Stacks source), blueprint-reviewer (whole-blueprint
+ HARD GATE), mathlib-analogist (api-alignment on the push–pull functor). Then acted on the

  sequence route as the project's dominant infeasibility risk (both SS absent from Mathlib for
  `Scheme.Modules`; Leray additionally needs quasi-coherence of `R^q f_*` via Mayer–Vietoris —
  another absent prerequisite the blueprint had omitted). The goal is only the weak
  `Nonempty (homology i ≅ rightDerived i)` existence form. The standard Cartan–Leray
  acyclic-cover proof (Stacks Tag 015E) gives it via ONE abstract homological-algebra lemma,

  and it REUSES the affine-acyclicity phase (P3) as its acyclicity input — removing a blocker
  and sharing infrastructure rather than building two SS from scratch.
- **LOC/risk trade-off**: Route B ≈ multi-thousand LOC of SS machinery + a Mayer–Vietoris
  quasi-coherence lemma; Route A ≈ one abstract lemma (~200–450 LOC) + the geometric inputs we
  needed anyway. Strictly lighter.
- **Cheapest reversal signal**: if the abstract lemma `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`
  proves unexpectedly hard in Lean (e.g. the comparison-of-resolutions route also hits a wall),
  reconsider Route B for the degree-0 / affine-base corollary only.

### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch

This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:

- For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
  `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
  the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
  chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
  chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
  its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
  `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
  checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
  gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
  consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
  against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
- If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
  finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
- Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
  finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
  blueprint depends on):
  1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
     same-iter fast path below).
  2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
     iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
     flagged.
  3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
     writer was asked to fix.
- **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
  the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
  scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
  must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
  `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
  gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
  prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
  still fails, F stays deferred.
- If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
  mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
  The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
  the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
  verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
  the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
  alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.

### Strategy / multi-route handling

- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
  or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
  blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
  in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
  until its blueprint coverage is in place.
- If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
  treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
  — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
  in the blueprint.

### What "deferred" means in practice

Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

### blueprint-writer

- Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
  review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
  present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
  multi-route coverage.
- Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
  matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
  enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
  explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
  what the directive lists.
- **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` markers** (no "after
  writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no "verification: confirm
  `\leanok` is present"). The writer's descriptor forbids it —
  ``\leanok`` is managed by the deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase. A
  directive that asks the writer to add ``\leanok`` puts it in a rule
  conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the "verification"
  instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok runs. Leave
  ``\leanok`` out of the writer's directive entirely.
- **``\mathlibok`` is the exception — you MAY direct it.** When a
  declaration the chapter depends on is provided by Mathlib as-is, it
  is good practice to have the writer author an explicit *Mathlib
  dependency anchor*: a block that states the Mathlib result (in the
  project's notation), carries ``\lean{}`` naming the real Mathlib
  declaration, and is marked ``\mathlibok`` so the DAG treats it as
  done and the route's reliance on Mathlib stays visible. Name such
  anchors in the directive (statement + the Mathlib ``\lean{}``
  target). The writer marks ``\mathlibok`` ONLY on these anchors —
  never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.
- **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
  the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
  writer with TWO globs:
    --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
    --write-domain 'references/**'
  The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
  body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
  reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
  `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
  need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
  are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
  parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
  route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
- After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
  in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
  confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
  the writer fixed everything.
- If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
  findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
  work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
  strategy-level issue.
- If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
  skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
  writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.

### dag-walker

- Dispatch me against a SEED declaration whose foundation you want made
  complete: an ∞-source (`archon dag-query gaps`), the project goal, or a
  frontier node you suspect has an incomplete `\uses{}` list. Name the seed
  label in the directive (`## Seed`). I walk UP from there.
- I am the "is the foundation under this target complete?" agent —
  complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise
  direction). I follow the dependency graph wherever it leads, across
  chapters, filling whatever the cone is missing. Give me a wide write
  domain when the cone spans chapters:
    --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex'
  and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a
  source (so I can spawn a reference-retriever).
- Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't
  trust: a node looks ready when its `\uses{}` under-declares its real
  dependencies. I close that gap so the injected frontier is honest.
- My report's "Could not complete" section lists dependencies that are
  genuinely hard/novel (no informal proof exists yet). Those are strategy
  items — read STRATEGY.md implications before sending a prover at them.
- **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** — that is the deterministic
  sync_leanok phase's job. `\mathlibok` I may add, but only on genuine
  Mathlib dependency anchors (see my body).

### effort-breaker

- Dispatch me against ONE target declaration that is too big to formalize in
  one go — a high-`effort_local` node, or one the progress-critic flags as a
  repeated blocker. I am most valuable on a **frontier** node (deps already
  done): it is the live bottleneck, and decomposing it exposes small ready
  pieces next iter.
- Name the target label in `## Target`, and say **how aggressively to break**
  in `## Granularity`: "one level" (the proof's main steps) by default, or
  "fine — one mathematical claim per lemma" when a prior, coarser break still
  left a hard piece. Decomposition is ITERATIVE and has no floor: if a break
  doesn't drop the difficulty enough, re-dispatch me on the still-hard
  sub-lemma asking me to break it further (sentence-by-sentence, like the
  fine-grained prover). Do not pre-empt this — just break, see if it helped,
  break more if not.
- Give me the proof sketch / reference structure to cut along in
  `## Proof structure` so I split at real mathematical seams, not arbitrary
  points.
- Do NOT dispatch me at an already-done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) — there
  is nothing to break. Do NOT dispatch me at an ∞-effort node (no informal
  proof to decompose) — send a dag-walker / blueprint-writer to write the
  proof first, then me to break it.
- **Never instruct me to add `\leanok`** (sync_leanok's job). `\mathlibok` only
  on genuine Mathlib anchors.

### mathlib-analogist

- I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or
  `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.
  Dispatch the right mode for the question:

  - **api-alignment**: "should this declaration be a typeclass / a
    structure / bundled / unbundled?", "does Mathlib already do this
    and we're building a parallel?". This is the default and matches
    every trigger below under "Proactive" and "Reactive".
  - **cross-domain-inspiration**: "this categorical pattern has been
    failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural
    problem in a different mathematical area?". Use the cross-domain
    triggers below.

- In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the
  same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would
  be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural
  analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you
  can port it.

### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)

Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:

- You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
  blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
  write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
  Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
- You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
  signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
  Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.
- The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is
  unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs
  unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).

### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)

Dispatch me when:

- Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a "parallel API"
  pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-
  modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).
- The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems
  wrong for downstream consumers.
- **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with "design-shape
  suspected" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective
  for design-related stuck routes — bridge lemmas multiplying
  around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's
  shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.

### Cross-domain inspiration triggers

Dispatch me in **cross-domain-inspiration** mode when:

- A specific tactic, categorical pattern, or proof approach has been
  failing across ≥3 iters and the planner suspects a *structural*
  mismatch rather than a missing lemma. The progress-critic's
  STUCK / CHURNING verdicts with "approach-shape suspected" as a
  root cause are explicit signals.
- The strategy-critic suggested an alternative route but you don't
  have a concrete starting shape — ask me to find precedents for
  the alternative in any Mathlib area.
- A new infrastructure construction is hard and you want to see
  whether Mathlib has solved the SAME categorical / topological /
  algebraic shape in a different domain (presheaf on schemes vs
  presheaf on topological spaces; pushout in algebra vs in
  geometry; descent in algebraic geometry vs in functional analysis;
  fixed-point theorems across measure theory / order theory /
  topology; ...).

In cross-domain-inspiration mode, your directive replaces the
api-alignment fields with:

- `## Structural problem` — the categorical / algebraic / topological
  shape you're trying to solve, stated as abstractly as possible
  ("we need a sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of
  modules", "we need a descent statement for a stack property along
  a flat surjection", ...).
- `## Failed approaches` — one short bullet per attempt that didn't
  work, with the underlying obstacle.
- `## Search radius` — `wide` (any Mathlib domain) | `narrow` (same
  general area as the project, but a different sub-area).

My output in cross-domain-inspiration mode is a ranked list of
structural analogues — each with: the Mathlib citation, the
technique used there, and a concrete suggestion for how to port it
to the project's setting. The planner uses this as an exploration
list, not as a directive.

### Strict severity

When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose
a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the
project's path works. "Works" is not the bar when the cost is API
fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume
the unified Mathlib version.

I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my "PROCEED" verdict as the
minimum; treat my "ALIGN WITH MATHLIB" verdict as a refactor
obligation, not a suggestion.

I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future
iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.

### progress-critic

- I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
  do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
  have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
  verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.

  **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
    - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
      or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
      skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
    - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
      signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
      added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
      CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
    - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
      sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
      is no trajectory to extrapolate from).

  Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
  with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
  open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
  re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
- My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
  progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
  The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
  judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
  corrective.

### Strict context discipline

Your directive must contain ONLY:
  - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
    iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
  - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
    sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
    (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
  - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
    and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
    so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
    K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
    `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
    whole strategy.
  - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
    this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
    operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
  - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.

Your directive MUST NOT include:
  - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
    soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
  - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
    reviewer's territory).
  - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
    above).

If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
it. My value depends on the narrow focus.

### Acting on my verdicts

Verdicts are per-route:

- **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
  prover round.
- **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
  shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
  corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
  structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
  matching subagent from the catalog.
- **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
  iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
- **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
  data). Proceed but watch.

**CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.

``\uses{...}`` targets that no ``\label{...}`` defines).

If the doctor reports findings, surface them in your session
``summary.md`` and ``recommendations.md`` so the next plan agent
knows to address them. If the report is missing or empty, that
means the doctor was either skipped or found nothing to flag.

## ``\leanok`` sync attribution

Before flagging any proof-block ``\leanok`` on a sorry-bodied
decl as headline laundering, consult:

  /home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology/.archon/sync_leanok-state.json

Schema: ``{iter, sha, timestamp, added, removed, chapters_touched}``.

- ``iter`` equals this iteration (004) ⇒ sync has run for
  the current tree. Any remaining ``\leanok`` is the script's
  deterministic verdict; only flag genuine laundering after a
  first-hand audit of the Lean source.
- ``iter`` is older or the file is missing ⇒ markers may be stale.
  Note the ambiguity in ``summary.md`` instead of raising CRITICAL.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Review Agent
2	
3	You analyze the most recent prover session, write a structured proof journal, update project status, maintain the semantic blueprint markers, and write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	## What you may and may not do
6	
7	- **You do NOT modify any `.lean` files**, write proofs, or fill sorries.
8	- **You do NOT touch `\leanok`**. The deterministic `sync_leanok` phase ran between the prover and you. If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the file has a sorry or doesn't compile — investigate, don't paper over.
9	- **You DO maintain the semantic markers**: `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections after renames, `% NOTE:` annotations, and stale `\notready` removal.
10	- **You may run** `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` for verification, and `sorry_analyzer` for per-file counts.
11	
12	## Iteration / session numbering
13	
14	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. These are the same number — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN`. Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) in prose; use the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory.
15	
16	## What the loop has pre-injected
17	
18	You do NOT need to "go read" any of the following — the content is already in your prompt:
19	
20	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
21	- **Subagent catalog** (the authoritative roster of available subagents for this phase — do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
22	- **Blueprint doctor report path** (its findings are also referenced by the plan agent's next iter; you should incorporate any structural issues into your summary).
23	
24	## Blueprint dependency graph (leandag)
25	
26	Ground your assessment in the actual dependency graph (the same leandag graph the dashboard DAG page and the planner use) rather than recollection. Query it read-only — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr):
27	
28	```
29	archon dag-query gaps --json        # ∞ holes: statements with no informal proof (roadmap blockers to surface)
30	archon dag-query frontier           # what is genuinely ready to prove right now
31	archon dag-query ancestors --node <label>   # a declaration's full dependency closure — is its foundation sound?
32	```
33	
34	Use it where it sharpens your judgment: surface a persistent ∞ hole in `TO_USER.md` (Step 7) when no in-loop fix is in reach; sanity-check that a declaration's `\uses{}` closure is complete before treating it as done; and when applying `\mathlibok` (Step 6), confirm the node really is a Mathlib-backed leaf. It is read-only — you never mutate the graph, only the blueprint markers your steps already own.
35	
36	## Step 1 — Identify context
37	
38	1. Note the session number from your invocation prompt (= iter number).
39	2. The current global sorry count is already in `meta.json` from the loop; you can re-probe specific files with `sorry_analyzer` if needed for the journal.
40	3. `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` for what changed.
41	
42	## Step 2 — Read pre-processed attempt data (MANDATORY)
43	
44	**Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` completely.** This is your PRIMARY data source — task_results files are supplementary.
45	
46	- Line 1: summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors).
47	- Other lines: one event per tool call (edits, goal states, diagnostics, builds).
48	
49	If the file's first line carries `"no_prover_lane": true`, the loop is signalling no prover ran this iter (intentional skip). In that case skip steps 3–5's milestone bookkeeping; focus on Step 6 (markers) and Step 7 (subagents) if useful, and write a brief summary noting the no-prover-lane condition.
50	
51	For each event:
52	- `code_change` → record the actual code tried (old_text → new_text).
53	- `goal_state` → record the Lean goal at that point.
54	- `diagnostics` → record the Lean errors.
55	- `build` → record success / failure.
56	
57	## Step 3 — Read recent history
58	
59	The recent-iter sidecars are already injected. If you need older sessions, read `summary.md` from the last 2 sessions on disk. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
60	
61	## Step 4 — Write the proof journal
62	
63	Create three files under `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/`:
64	
65	### `summary.md`
66	
67	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted).
68	- For each target: every significant attempt (tactic / code tried, Lean error, goal state at that point); what was learned; for solved targets, the final proof structure with key lemmas.
69	- Key findings / patterns discovered.
70	- Recommendations for the next session.
71	
72	### `milestones.jsonl`
73	
74	One JSON object per line, one entry per target theorem:
75	
76	```json
77	{
78	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
79	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
80	  "target": {"file": "path/to/File.lean", "theorem": "theorem_name"},
81	  "session": {"id": "session_N", "model": "model-name"},
82	  "findings": {"blocker": "if blocked", "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1"]},
83	  "attempts": [{
84	    "attempt": 1, "strategy": "what was tried", "code_tried": "Lean code or tactic",
85	    "line_number": "line where attempted",
86	    "lean_error": "error message if failed",
87	    "goal_before": "...", "goal_after": "...",
88	    "result": "success|failed|partial", "insight": "what was learned"
89	  }],
90	  "next_steps": "..."
91	}
92	```
93	
94	The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from `attempts_raw.jsonl`. Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one with `code_tried` + `lean_error`.
95	
96	### `recommendations.md`
97	
98	Concrete next-plan-iter recommendations:
99	- Closest-to-completion targets to prioritize.
100	- Promising approaches needing more work.
101	- Blocked targets and why (plan agent should NOT re-assign these).
102	- Reusable proof patterns discovered.
103	
104	If the prover has hit the same blocker for several consecutive iters on the same target, explicitly tell the plan agent to NOT retry the same approach without a structural change first.
105	
106	## Step 5 — Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
107	
108	PROJECT_STATUS.md carries the cumulative Knowledge Base only — per-session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
109	
110	```markdown
111	# Project Status
112	
113	## Knowledge Base
114	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
115	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
116	
117	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
118	- <target>: <reason>
119	
120	## Last Updated
121	<ISO timestamp>
122	```
123	
124	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries a legacy "Overall Progress" section, leave that content where it is (don't delete history) but stop appending to it.
125	
126	### Per-iter sidecar split write
127	
128	The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to **`iter/iter-NNN/review.md`** — born-bounded, one file per iter. The Knowledge Base in PROJECT_STATUS.md is the only growing-but-curated part.
129	
130	## Step 6 — Blueprint markers (semantic only)
131	
132	`\leanok` is owned by `sync_leanok`. **Your domain is the markers that require semantic judgement:**
133	
134	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement block only) — add when the Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`) AND the Archon-side declaration has no sorry and introduces no new proof obligation. The deterministic script never adds/removes this.
135	- **`\lean{...}` corrections** — when a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, the task result will mention it. Update the chapter's `\lean{...}` to the correct name.
136	- **`% NOTE: <reason>`** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, annotate with a `% NOTE: ...` so the plan agent sees it.
137	- **Stale `\notready`** — strip when the prover has landed the block.
138	- **1-to-1 coverage debt** — run `archon dag-query unmatched --json`: every `lean_aux` node is a Lean declaration (usually a prover-created helper from this session) with **no blueprint entry**, invisible to the dependency graph. You do not write informal prose, so do NOT author the entries yourself — instead list each one in `recommendations.md` (file, declaration name, and what its Lean proof depends on, read from the source) so the planner restores the correspondence next iteration. The doctrine: when there is Lean there must be tex, even for trivial helpers.
139	
140	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave proof-less blocks alone.
141	
142	In `summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated (manual)" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic `sync_leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]`):
143	
144	```markdown

8	/-!
9	# Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors
10	
11	This file provides the abstract homological-algebra core underlying the Čech
12	computation of higher direct images (Stacks Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma):
13	**an acyclic resolution computes every right-derived functor**.
14	
15	Throughout, `𝒜` and `ℬ` are abelian categories, `𝒜` has injective resolutions
16	(so that `G.rightDerived n` is everywhere defined), and `G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ` is an
17	additive functor.
18	
19	## Declarations
20	
21	* `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` — typeclass for right-`G`-acyclic objects.
22	* Instance: every injective object is right-acyclic (from Mathlib's
23	  `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`).
24	
25	The following declarations are outlined in the strategy block below and will be
26	constructed by the prover in `mathlib-build` mode:
27	
28	* `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` — dual Horseshoe Lemma:
29	  lift `0 → A → B → C → 0` to a degreewise-split SES of injective resolutions.
30	* `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` — dimension-shift
31	  isomorphism `(Rᵏ G)(Z) ≅ (Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(A)` across a SES with acyclic middle term.
32	* `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` — main theorem:
33	  `(Rⁿ G)(A) ≅ Hⁿ(G(J•))` for any acyclic resolution `J•` of `A`.
34	
35	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_AcyclicResolution.tex` and
36	`.archon/analogies/p4-derived-les.md` for the full informal argument.

38	## Mathlib building blocks (all verified present)
39	
40	All from `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/RightDerived.lean`:
41	- `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj` — iso
42	  `(F.rightDerived n).obj X ≅ Hⁿ(F.mapHomologicalComplex.obj I.cocomplex)`.
43	- `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf` — `R⁰G ≅ G` (left-exact `G`).
44	- `CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` — vanishing on
45	  injectives: `IsZero ((F.rightDerived (n+1)).obj J)` for `[Injective J]`.

47	From `Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean`:
48	- `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁`
49	- `CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂`

63	namespace CategoryTheory
64	
65	variable {𝒜 : Type*} [Category 𝒜] [Abelian 𝒜] [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜]

68	/-!
69	### Right-acyclic objects
70	Blueprint: `def:right_acyclic` (§ "Right-acyclic objects").
71	-/
72	
73	/-- An object `J : 𝒜` is *right-`G`-acyclic* when every higher right-derived
74	functor of `G` vanishes at `J`:
75	```
76	(Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(J) = 0   for all k : ℕ.
77	```
78	The index-shifted quantifier `k + 1` matches the statement of
79	`Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` and avoids an inequality
80	side-condition; it is equivalent to `(Rⁿ G)(J) = 0` for all `n ≥ 1`.
81	
82	Blueprint: `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic` (`def:right_acyclic`).
83	-/
84	class Functor.IsRightAcyclic (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) : Prop where
85	  vanish : ∀ k : ℕ, Limits.IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k + 1)).obj J)
86	
87	/-- Every injective object is right-`G`-acyclic.
88	Follows immediately from `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ`. -/
89	instance (priority := 100) Functor.IsRightAcyclic.ofInjective
90	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] (J : 𝒜) [Injective J] : Functor.IsRightAcyclic G J where
91	  vanish k := Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ G k J
92	-- Note: `Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ` returns
93	-- `Limits.IsZero ((G.rightDerived (k+1)).obj J)`, matching the class field.

95	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — acyclic resolutions
96	
97	The declarations in this section are project-local infrastructure feeding the
98	dimension-shift and acyclic-resolution comparison theorems (Stacks Tag 015D/015E).
99	They are not yet in Mathlib. -/
100	
101	open Limits
102	
103	/-- The cohomology of the `G`-image of an injective resolution of a right-`G`-acyclic
104	object `J` vanishes in every positive degree. This is the homology-level form of
105	right-acyclicity, obtained by transporting the vanishing of `(R^{k+1} G)(J)` across
106	`InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj`. It is the input that kills the middle terms
107	of the homology long exact sequence in the dimension-shift step. -/
108	lemma Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic
109	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] {J : 𝒜} (I : InjectiveResolution J)
110	    [G.IsRightAcyclic J] (k : ℕ) :
111	    IsZero ((HomologicalComplex.homologyFunctor ℬ (ComplexShape.up ℕ) (k + 1)).obj
112	      ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj I.cocomplex)) :=
113	  (Functor.IsRightAcyclic.vanish (G := G) (J := J) k).of_iso
114	    (I.isoRightDerivedObj G (k + 1)).symm
115	
116	omit [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜] in
117	/-- A short complex of cochain complexes that is *degreewise split* (each degree carries a
118	`ShortComplex.Splitting`) is short exact. Project-local because Mathlib only provides the
119	degreewise-short-exact criterion `shortExact_of_degreewise_shortExact`; this packages the
120	common special case where the degreewise data is a splitting. -/
121	lemma shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting
122	    {S : ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ)}
123	    (splits : ∀ n, (S.map (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting) :
124	    S.ShortExact :=
125	  HomologicalComplex.shortExact_of_degreewise_shortExact S (fun n => (splits n).shortExact)
126	
127	omit [HasInjectiveResolutions 𝒜] in
128	/-- An additive functor applied degreewise to a degreewise-split short complex of cochain
129	complexes yields a short exact short complex. This is the step where degreewise splitness is
130	essential: `G` is not assumed exact, but it preserves the *split* short exact sequences in each
131	degree (`ShortComplex.Splitting.map`), and degreewise short exactness then assembles to a short
132	exact sequence of complexes. -/
133	lemma shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting
134	    {S : ShortComplex (CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ)}
135	    (splits : ∀ n, (S.map (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting)
136	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] :

141	/-- **Dimension shift, part (1), from a degreewise-split SES of injective resolutions.**
142	Given a short exact sequence `0 → A → J → Z → 0` lifted (via the horseshoe) to a
143	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions
144	`0 → I_A → I_J → I_Z → 0`, presented here as chain maps `φ, ψ` with degreewise splittings,
145	and with the middle object `J` right-`G`-acyclic, the connecting map of the homology long
146	exact sequence of `G(I_•)` is an isomorphism in every positive degree:
147	`(R^{k+1} G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+2} G)(A)`.
148	
149	This is the engine of the staircase induction (`rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution`).
150	It is stated over the *resolution-level* SES because the object-level dimension-shift theorem
151	`rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic` requires the horseshoe lift to produce that SES; once the
152	horseshoe is available, the object-level statement follows by feeding its output here. -/
153	noncomputable def Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES
154	    (G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ) [G.Additive] {A J Z : 𝒜}
155	    (I_A : InjectiveResolution A) (I_J : InjectiveResolution J) (I_Z : InjectiveResolution Z)
156	    [G.IsRightAcyclic J]
157	    (φ : I_A.cocomplex ⟶ I_J.cocomplex) (ψ : I_J.cocomplex ⟶ I_Z.cocomplex)
158	    (w : φ ≫ ψ = 0)
159	    (splits : ∀ n, ((ShortComplex.mk φ ψ w).map
160	      (HomologicalComplex.eval 𝒜 (ComplexShape.up ℕ) n)).Splitting)
161	    (k : ℕ) :
162	    (G.rightDerived (k + 1)).obj Z ≅ (G.rightDerived (k + 2)).obj A :=
163	  have hSG : ((ShortComplex.mk φ ψ w).map
164	      (G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ))).ShortExact :=
165	    shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting splits G
166	  (I_Z.isoRightDerivedObj G (k + 1)) ≪≫
167	    hSG.δIso (k + 1) (k + 2) (by simp)
168	      (G.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic I_J k)
169	      (G.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic I_J (k + 1)) ≪≫
170	    (I_A.isoRightDerivedObj G (k + 2)).symm

173	/-- **Horseshoe per-stage monomorphism.** Given an exact short complex
174	`A → B → C` with `A → B` a monomorphism, and monomorphisms `α : A ↪ P`, `γ : C ↪ Q` with `P`
175	injective, the map `B → P ⊞ Q` whose first component is the injective extension of `α` along
176	`A ↪ B` (`Injective.factorThru α S.f`) and whose second component is `B ↠ C ↪ Q` is itself a
177	monomorphism. This is the cokernel/kernel step driving each stage of the dual Horseshoe Lemma
178	`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`: applied to the `n`-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with
179	`P = I_A.X (n+1)`, `Q = I_C.X (n+1)`, it produces the monomorphism into the next biproduct term
180	whose cokernel feeds the following stage. -/
181	lemma mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact {S : ShortComplex 𝒜} (hS : S.Exact) [Mono S.f]
182	    {P Q : 𝒜} [Injective P] (α : S.X₁ ⟶ P) [Mono α] (γ : S.X₃ ⟶ Q) [Mono γ] :
183	    Mono (biprod.lift (Injective.factorThru α S.f) (S.g ≫ γ)) := by
184	  rw [Preadditive.mono_iff_cancel_zero]
185	  intro T x hx
186	  have h1 : x ≫ Injective.factorThru α S.f = 0 := by simpa using hx =≫ biprod.fst
187	  have h2 : x ≫ S.g ≫ γ = 0 := by simpa using hx =≫ biprod.snd
188	  have hxg : x ≫ S.g = 0 := by rw [← cancel_mono γ, zero_comp, Category.assoc]; exact h2
189	  have hyf : hS.lift x hxg ≫ S.f = x := hS.lift_f x hxg
190	  have hya : hS.lift x hxg ≫ α = 0 := by
191	    have h3 : hS.lift x hxg ≫ S.f ≫ Injective.factorThru α S.f = 0 := by
192	      rw [← Category.assoc, hyf]; exact h1
193	    rwa [Injective.comp_factorThru] at h3
194	  have hy0 : hS.lift x hxg = 0 := by rw [← cancel_mono α, zero_comp]; exact hya
195	  rw [← hyf, hy0, zero_comp]

201	(`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`): a degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective

205	* `Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` — given the horseshoe SES (as chain maps
206	  `φ, ψ` with degreewise splittings), produces the dimension-shift iso
207	  `(R^{k+1} G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+2} G)(A)` directly (no further homological algebra needed). This is
208	  TARGET 2 part (1), modulo plugging in the horseshoe.
209	* `shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting` /
210	  `shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting` — turn the degreewise splittings
211	  into complex-level `ShortExact`, before and after applying the additive `G`.
212	* `Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic` — kills the acyclic middle
213	  terms `H^{≥1}(G(I_J))` (the δIso vanishing hypotheses).
214	* `mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact` — the per-stage monomorphism `B → I_A^{n+1} ⊞ I_C^{n+1}`

243	TARGET 1: `CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact`
244	Blueprint: `lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` (§ "Lifting a SES to injective resolutions").
245	
246	Statement (informal):
247	  Given `0 → A → B → C → 0` short exact in `𝒜`, produce injective resolutions
248	  `I_A`, `I_B`, `I_C` and a degreewise-split short exact sequence of cochain complexes
249	  `0 → I_A.cocomplex → I_B.cocomplex → I_C.cocomplex → 0`.

251	Construction (dual Horseshoe Lemma):
252	1. Choose resolutions `I_A : InjectiveResolution A` and `I_C : InjectiveResolution C`
253	   (they exist because `𝒜` has injective resolutions / enough injectives).
254	2. Set `I_B^n := I_A.cocomplex.X n ⊞ I_C.cocomplex.X n` (biproduct of injectives
255	   = injective). The inclusion `I_A^n ↪ I_B^n` and projection `I_B^n ↠ I_C^n`
256	   are the canonical biproduct maps, so every degree is degreewise split.
257	3. Build the augmentation `B → I_B^0` and the twisted differential
258	   `d_B : I_B^n → I_B^{n+1}` by induction on `n`:
259	   - At each stage, the off-diagonal component `τ_n : I_C^n → I_A^{n+1}` is
260	     obtained by applying the injective lifting property
261	     (`Injective.factorThru`) to the mono `I_A^{n+1} → ...` and the relevant
262	     composite through `B`.
263	   - Differential in matrix notation: `d_B = [[d_A, τ], [0, d_C]]`.

265	5. Prove `I_B` resolves `B`: exactness follows from the homology LES
266	   (`ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁/₂/₃`) applied to the degreewise-split
267	   SES of complexes (split ⇒ additive functor preserves ⇒ applies to any `G`);
268	   the outer complexes `I_A, I_C` are acyclic, so the LES forces `I_B` acyclic.
269	
270	Mathlib models (read these files):
271	- `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Abelian/Injective/Resolution.lean:270–352`
272	  (`InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex`, `exact_f_d`, `ofCocomplex_exactAt_succ`)
273	- `Injective.factorThru` for the stage-by-stage lift.
274	
275	Build advice: do NOT write one monolithic def. Instead chain:
276	  a) `horseshoe_τ (n : ℕ) : I_C.cocomplex.X n ⟶ I_A.cocomplex.X (n+1)` — off-diagonal.

278	  c) Exactness lemmas per degree.

292	────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
293	TARGET 2: `CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic`
294	Blueprint: `lem:acyclic_dimension_shift` (§ "The dimension-shift step").
295	
296	Statement (informal):
297	  Let `G : 𝒜 ⥤ ℬ` be additive and left-exact, and
298	  `0 → A → J → Z → 0` a SES in `𝒜` with `J` right-`G`-acyclic. Then:
299	  (1) Connecting isomorphisms: `(Rᵏ G)(Z) ≅ (Rᵏ⁺¹ G)(A)` for all `k ≥ 1`.
300	  (2) Base case: `(R¹ G)(A) ≅ coker(G(J) → G(Z))`.
301	
302	Route:
303	1. Apply `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` to get a degreewise-split SES
304	   `0 → I_A → I_J → I_Z → 0` of injective resolutions.
305	2. Apply `G` degreewise. Degreewise splitness + additive `G` ⇒ the image SES
306	   `0 → G(I_A) → G(I_J) → G(I_Z) → 0` is also short exact (as complexes).
307	   (Use `ShortComplex.Splitting.map` or `Functor.mapShortComplex` on the per-degree
308	   splitting.)
309	3. Feed to `ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁/₂/₃` and `ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ`
310	   to get the complex-level homology LES.
311	4. Transport each `Hⁿ(G(I_•))` to `(Rⁿ G)(•)` via
312	   `InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj` (for `I_A`, `I_J`, `I_Z` individually).
313	5. Use `[IsRightAcyclic G J]` (i.e. `Functor.IsRightAcyclic.vanish`) to kill the
314	   `(Rᵏ G)(J)` terms for `k ≥ 1`; exactness collapses the LES to the isomorphisms
315	   claimed in parts (1) and (2).

335	   `(Rⁿ G)(A) ≅ (Rⁿ⁻¹ G)(Z¹) ≅ ⋯ ≅ (R¹ G)(Zⁿ⁻¹)`
336	   and close with part (2) applied to the `(n-1)`-th SES.

11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}

92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}

126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
191	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
192	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
193	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
194	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
195	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
196	  \[
197	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
198	  \]
199	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
200	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
201	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
202	  \[
203	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
204	  \]
205	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
206	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
207	  \[
208	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
209	      \longrightarrow 0
210	  \]
211	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
212	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
213	\end{lemma}
214	
215	\begin{proof}
216	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
217	  \leanok
218	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma; see e.g.\ Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
219	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8 (stated there for projective resolutions; the
220	  injective-resolution form is the dual). Choose injective resolutions
221	  \(A \to I_A^\bullet\) and \(C \to I_C^\bullet\), which exist because \(\mathcal{A}\)
222	  has enough injectives. Set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) in each degree; this is
223	  injective, being a finite direct sum of injectives. The maps
224	  \(I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and \(I_B^n \to I_C^n\) are the canonical coproduct inclusion
225	  and product projection, so each degree is split exact by construction. It remains
226	  to build the augmentation \(B \to I_B^0\) and the differentials
227	  \(I_B^n \to I_B^{n+1}\) making \(I_B^\bullet\) a resolution of \(B\) and the two
228	  vertical families chain maps. One proceeds degree by degree: at each stage the
229	  required lift exists because the relevant target summand \(I_A^{\bullet}\) is
230	  injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism, so the universal lifting
231	  property of injectives produces the missing component; the diagonal differential of
232	  \(I_B^\bullet\) is then assembled from the differentials of \(I_A^\bullet\),
233	  \(I_C^\bullet\) and these lifts. Exactness of the resulting \(I_B^\bullet\) (that it
234	  resolves \(B\)) follows from the long exact homology sequence
235	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to the degreewise-split short
236	  exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet \to 0\): the
237	  outer complexes are acyclic resolutions, so the connecting maps force
238	  \(I_B^\bullet\) to be acyclic in positive degrees and to have \(H^0 = B\).
239	\end{proof}
