241	\section{The dimension-shift step}
242	
243	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
244	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
245	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
246	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
247	complex-level homology long exact sequence
248	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
249	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
250	sub-lemma below.
251	
252	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
253	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
254	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
255	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
256	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
257	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
258	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
259	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
260	  % between abelian categories and assume
261	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
262	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
263	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
264	  % \begin{enumerate}
265	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
266	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
267	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
268	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
269	  % \end{enumerate}
270	  % In each of the three cases
271	  % $$
272	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
273	  % $$
274	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
275	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
276	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
277	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
278	  \[
279	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
280	  \]
281	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
282	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
283	  \begin{enumerate}
284	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
285	      furnish isomorphisms
286	      \[
287	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
288	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
289	      \]
290	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
291	      \[
292	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
293	      \]
294	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
295	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
296	  \end{enumerate}
297	\end{lemma}
298	
299	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
300	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
301	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
302	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
303	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
304	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
305	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
306	\begin{proof}
307	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
308	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
309	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
310	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
311	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
312	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
313	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
314	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
315	
316	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
317	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
318	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
319	  complexes
320	  \[
321	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
322	  \]
323	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
324	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
325	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
326	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
327	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
328	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
329	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
330	  \[
331	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
332	      \longrightarrow 0
333	  \]
334	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
335	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
336	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
337	  \[
338	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
339	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
340	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
341	  \]
342	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
343	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
344	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
345	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
346	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
347	  sequence of right-derived functors
348	  \[
349	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
350	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
351	  \]
352	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
353	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
354	  \[
355	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
356	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
357	  \]
358	
359	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
360	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
361	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
362	  \[
363	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
364	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
365	  \]
366	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
367	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
368	  In the lowest degree the segment
369	  \[
370	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
371	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
372	  \]
373	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
374	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
375	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
376	  part (2).
377	\end{proof}
378	
379	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
380	
381	\begin{theorem}[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
382	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
383	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
384	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
385	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
386	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
387	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
388	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
389	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
390	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
391	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
392	  % L5785--5811).
393	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
394	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
395	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
396	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
397	  % $$
398	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
399	  % $$
400	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
401	  % $RF$."
402	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
403	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
404	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
405	  % and we obtain an exact functor
406	  % $$
407	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
408	  % $$
409	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
410	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
411	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
412	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
413	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
414	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
415	  Let
416	  \[
417	    0 \longrightarrow A
418	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
419	      \longrightarrow \cdots
420	  \]
421	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
422	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
423	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
424	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
425	  \[
426	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
427	  \]
428	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
429	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
430	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
431	  object.
432	\end{theorem}
433	
434	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
435	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
436	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
437	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
438	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
439	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
440	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
441	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
442	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
443	% the long exact cohomology sequence
444	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
445	% to an exact sequence
446	% $$
447	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
448	% $$
449	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
450	\begin{proof}
451	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
452	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
453	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
454	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
455	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
456	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
457	  resolution into the
458	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
459	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
460	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
461	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
462	  \[
463	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
464	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
465	  \]
466	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
467	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
468	
469	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
470	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
471	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
472	  \[
473	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
474	      = (R^0 G)(A),
475	  \]
476	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
477	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
478	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
479	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
480	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
481	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
482	  \[
483	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
484	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
485	  \]
486	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
487	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
488	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
489	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
490	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
491	
492	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
493	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
494	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
495	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
496	  \[
497	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
498	  \]
499	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
500	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
501	  isomorphisms down the staircase
502	  \[
503	    (R^n G)(A)
504	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
505	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
506	      \;\cong\; \cdots
507	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
508	  \]
509	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
510	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
511	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
512	  \[
513	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
514	  \]
515	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
516	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
517	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
518	  \[
519	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
520	      \;\cong\;
521	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
522	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
523	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
524	  \]
525	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
526	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
527	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
528	
529	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
530	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
531	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
532	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
533	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
534	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
535	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
536	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
537	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
538	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
539	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
540	\end{proof}

# Session 4 (iter-004) — review summary

## Metadata
- **Iteration / session**: iter-004 / session_4
- **Prover lane**: one (P4 → `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean`, `[prover-mode: mathlib-build]`). Model: sonnet.
- **Global sorry count**: 2 → 2 (unchanged). Remaining sorries both in `CechHigherDirectImage.lean`: `CechAcyclic.affine` (L774), `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` (L811) — P3/P5, intentionally out of scope this iter.
- **`AcyclicResolution.lean`**: 0 → 0 sorries (`mathlib-build` adds *new* sorry-free declarations; does not touch existing sorries).
- **Declarations added: 5**, all verified axiom-clean (`{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`).
- **Named blueprint targets completed: 0 of 3** (horseshoe / object-level shift / staircase all remain).

## What the prover did (P4 lane: Stacks 015E, acyclic resolution computes R^nG)

The lane is a single bottom-up chain: horseshoe → dimension-shift → staircase. This session built
**every piece that *consumes* the horseshoe** and verified all Mathlib inputs are present, collapsing
the whole lane to one remaining construction. The five new declarations (all RESOLVED, axiom-clean):

1. `Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic` (L108) — `Hᵏ⁺¹(G(I_J))=0` for
   acyclic `J`, by transporting `IsRightAcyclic.vanish` across `isoRightDerivedObj`. Kills the middle
   terms feeding `δIso`.
2. `shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting` (L121) — degreewise `ShortComplex.Splitting` ⇒ complex-level
   `ShortExact`. Wrapper over `HomologicalComplex.shortExact_of_degreewise_shortExact`.
3. `shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting` (L133) — additive `G` preserves a
   degreewise-*split* SES of complexes. Key defeq: `G.mapHomologicalComplex c ⋙ eval n = eval n ⋙ G`.
4. `Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES` (L153) — **the dimension-shift engine**, at the
   resolution level. Given the horseshoe SES *as a hypothesis* (chain maps `φ,ψ`, degreewise `splits`,
   acyclic middle `J`), produces `(R^{k+1}G)(Z) ≅ (R^{k+2}G)(A)` as a 3-step `Iso.trans`:
   `isoRightDerivedObj(Z) ≪≫ δIso ≪≫ isoRightDerivedObj(A).symm`.
5. `mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact` (L181) — the per-stage horseshoe monomorphism
   `B ↪ I_A^{n+1} ⊞ I_C^{n+1}`. Needs only `Exact + Mono S.f` (epi unused), so it applies verbatim to
   every cosyzygy stage.

### Key finding — the dimension shift is far cheaper than the strategy feared
The strategy (STRATEGY.md / iter-002–003) treated the SES→LES propagation for `rightDerived` as a
to-build kernel requiring a hand-rolled long-exact-sequence chase. In fact Mathlib's
`ShortComplex.ShortExact.δIso` (`Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/HomologySequence.lean:355`) yields the
connecting iso `Hⁱ(X₃) ≅ Hʲ(X₁)` *directly* from `IsZero` of the middle homology in the two adjacent
degrees. Combined with `isoRightDerivedObj`, the entire dimension shift is a three-step `Iso.trans` —
no LES chase, no horseshoe needed for *this* step. This is a reusable pattern (see Knowledge Base).

### Why the prover stopped
The sole adjacent declaration left is the horseshoe object `InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` itself:
a monolithic ℕ-recursion building `I_B.cocomplex` with twisted differential `[[d_A,τ],[0,d_C]]` plus a
proof that `I_B` resolves `B`. `mathlib-build` permits no `sorry`, and there is no smaller
self-contained axiom-clean fragment beyond decl #5 (already landed). The informal-agent fallback was
unavailable (only `GEMINI_CLI_*` in env, which `archon-informal-agent.py` does not consume).

## HEADLINE FINDING — false `\leanok` on the horseshoe (DAG poisoning)

`lem:injective_resolution_of_ses` (the horseshoe, `\lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}`)
carries `\leanok` on **both** its statement block (chapter L190) and its proof block (chapter L217),
but **`InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact` does not exist as a Lean declaration** (`lean_verify`:
"Unknown constant"). Confirmed first-hand and independently by the lean-vs-blueprint-checker.

**Root cause**: the `/-! ... -/` strategy comment in `AcyclicResolution.lean` (lines ~230–347) contains
a ``` ```-fenced "Output type (suggested)" snippet whose first line is, literally,
`def InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact {A B C : 𝒜} (ses : ShortComplex 𝒜)` (file L283). The
`sync_leanok` declaration matcher (and the LSP outline) read that fenced text as a real, sorry-free
declaration and stamped `\leanok`. `sync_leanok-state.json` for iter-004 records `added: 3, removed: 0`
on exactly this chapter — almost certainly `def:right_acyclic` (genuine) + the two false markers above.

**Why it matters**: the horseshoe is the *sole remaining P4 blocker*. A false "done" on it tells the
DAG/planner the hardest piece is built, risking the planner never dispatching a prover to construct it.
This is a structural false-done, not a cosmetic marker slip.

**Remediation** (I cannot touch `\leanok` — owned by `sync_leanok` — nor edit `.lean`): I added a
`% NOTE:` to the block (chapter L190) documenting the false marker and the fix. The actual fix is
Lean-side and belongs to the next plan iter: reformat the code fence in the strategy comment (prose or
`--` prefix) so the matcher stops matching; `sync_leanok` then strips both `\leanok` automatically.
See `recommendations.md` (top) and `task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-acyclic.md`.

## Subagent dispatches
